Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(rvest)
library(wordcloud)
library(tm)
library(tidytext)
library(SnowballC)
library(ggplot2)

Romeo and Juliet

“Oh Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?”

Full script - http://shakespeare.mit.edu/romeo_juliet/full.html

Let’s explore…

  1. Whose name appears more often, Romeo or Juliet?
  2. Perform “pre-cleaning” on the file to remove extraneous characters.
  3. Identify which word appears most often in the text

Read in Romeo and Juliet

Code 
RandJ0 <- read_file("/Users/donovandean/Documents/Academics/Villanova University/Spring 2026/Data Science Tools (STAT8796)/Assignments/Homework/HW4/HW4 Instructions_files/RandJ.txt")

Term Frequency

Whose name appears more often, Romeo or Juliet?{r}

Code 
RandJ_format <- str_to_lower(RandJ0)
RandJ_remove_speaking <- RandJ_format |> 
  str_remove_all("\\*romeo\\*|\\*juliet\\*")

romeo_count <- str_count(RandJ_remove_speaking, "\\bromeo\\b") |> 
  sum()
juliet_count <- str_count(RandJ_remove_speaking, "\\bjuliet\\b") |> 
  sum()

tibble(name = c("Romeo", "Juliet"),
       frequency = c(romeo_count, juliet_count))
# A tibble: 2 × 2
  name   frequency
  <chr>      <int>
1 Romeo        146
2 Juliet        63

After removing the speaker labels so that only textual mentions were counted, Romeo (146) appears more frequently than Juliet (63) in the play.

Pre-cleaning / Tokenize

Perform “pre-cleaning” on the file to remove extraneous characters.

Code 
RandJ_tbl <- tibble(text = RandJ_remove_speaking)
RandJ_words <- RandJ_tbl |> 
  unnest_tokens(word, text)

RandJ_words_clean <- RandJ_words |> 
  filter(str_detect(word, "^[a-z]+$"))
head(RandJ_words_clean, 20)
# A tibble: 20 × 1
   word      
   <chr>     
 1 act       
 2 i         
 3 prologue  
 4 two       
 5 households
 6 both      
 7 alike     
 8 in        
 9 dignity   
10 in        
11 fair      
12 verona    
13 where     
14 we        
15 lay       
16 our       
17 scene     
18 from      
19 ancient   
20 grudge
Code 
data(stop_words)
RandJ_words_nostop <- RandJ_words_clean |> 
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word")
head(RandJ_words_nostop, 20)
# A tibble: 20 × 1
   word      
   <chr>     
 1 act       
 2 prologue  
 3 households
 4 alike     
 5 dignity   
 6 fair      
 7 verona    
 8 lay       
 9 scene     
10 ancient   
11 grudge    
12 break     
13 mutiny    
14 civil     
15 blood     
16 makes     
17 civil     
18 hands     
19 unclean   
20 fatal
Code 
word_count <- RandJ_words_nostop |> 
  count(word, sort = TRUE)
head(word_count, 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   word         n
   <chr>    <int>
 1 thou       277
 2 thy        167
 3 nurse      146
 4 thee       139
 5 love       138
 6 capulet    135
 7 romeo      130
 8 lady       106
 9 friar       97
10 mercutio    83

Wordcloud

In case we get here during in-class coding and you want to access what I have, here are two rds files…that I’ll talk about when we get here.

Code 
set.seed(123)

wordcloud(words = word_count$word,
          freq = word_count$n,
          max.words = 100)

n-grams

Code 
RandJ_bigrams <- RandJ_tbl |> 
  unnest_tokens(bigrams, text, token = "ngrams", n = 2)

RandJ_bigrams_sep <- RandJ_bigrams |> 
  separate(bigrams, into = c("word1", "word2", sep = " "))

RandJ_bigrams_clean <- RandJ_bigrams_sep |> 
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word,
         !word2 %in% stop_words$word)

RandJ_bigrams_final <- RandJ_bigrams_clean |> 
  unite(bigrams, word1, word2, sep = " ")
bigram_count <- RandJ_bigrams_final |> 
  count(bigrams, sort = TRUE)
head(bigram_count, 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   bigrams            n
   <chr>          <int>
 1 friar laurence    73
 2 lady capulet      57
 3 thou art          28
 4 art thou          22
 5 thou hast         21
 6 thou wilt         21
 7 exeunt scene      16
 8 enter romeo       11
 9 ne er             10
10 enter capulet      9

Visualization of n-grams

Code 
bigram_count |> 
  slice_max(n, n = 10) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(bigrams, n), y = n)) + 
  geom_col() + 
  coord_flip() + 
  labs(title = "Top 10 Bigrams in Romeo and Juliet",
       x = NULL,
       y = "Count")

The most frequent bigram is “friar laurence”, reflecting the central role of Friar Laurence in the plot.
